stem:
You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 4-month-old girl brought to a metropolitan emergency department by her mother and the mother's partner after a witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure lasting four minutes that resolved before arrival. She was previously well, immunisations are up to date, and she is exclusively breastfed.

On examination she is afebrile and alert with a normal cry. Anterior fontanelle is soft and flat. There is a 2 cm bluish bruise over the left cheek and a small bruise on the left pinna. Her mother explains the baby rolled off the change table this morning while she was answering the door, and the mother's partner is the primary daytime carer while father is on a fly-in fly-out roster. Coagulation profile, capillary glucose, ammonia and electrolytes are within normal limits.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 138/min, BP 86/52 mmHg, RR 36/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert, well perfused; left cheek bruise 2 cm, small left pinna bruise; anterior fontanelle soft; no other surface marks; tone normal
data_table.Investigations: BSL 4.6 mmol/L; sodium, urea, creatinine within normal limits; INR within normal limits; ammonia within normal limits

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Non contrast CT head and skeletal survey, then notify child protection services
options.rationale: In a less than 6 month old with unexplained bruising plus a seizure, urgent neuroimaging plus skeletal survey is mandated and statutory notification is non-discretionary in every Australian jurisdiction. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse; RACP Position Statement)
source_refs: RACP Position Statement on Child Abuse

options.text: Discharge home with safety net advice and paediatric outpatient follow up in two weeks
options.rationale: A bruise in a non-cruising infant is a sentinel injury for abusive head trauma until proven otherwise; discharging without imaging or notification breaches the RCH child protection pathway. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)

options.text: Outpatient EEG and developmental review through the local community paediatrician
options.rationale: Outpatient EEG addresses a recurrent seizure workup but ignores the inflicted injury pattern; priority is excluding intracranial haemorrhage and occult fractures, not characterising epilepsy. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)

options.text: Admit overnight for observation only, with discharge to the mother in the morning
options.rationale: Observation alone neither investigates the injury pattern nor protects the child on discharge; notification and imaging cannot be deferred to morning rounds. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)

options.text: Request a metabolic screen including plasma amino acids and urine organic acids
options.rationale: Metabolic screen is appropriate for unexplained encephalopathy with abnormal gas or glucose; with normal glucose, gas and ammonia plus an explanatory injury pattern this misdirects the workup. (Source: RCH CPG: Hypoglycaemia)

explanation.summary: Any bruise in a pre-cruising infant must trigger an abusive head trauma workup because the positive predictive value of bruising in a non-mobile child for inflicted injury is extremely high. The RCH pathway pairs urgent non contrast CT head with a skeletal survey within 24 hours, ophthalmology review for retinal haemorrhages, and same-day statutory notification. A normal coagulation profile and metabolic screen do not exclude inflicted injury and must not delay imaging. The treating clinician makes the report; consent from the carer is not required.
key_points: Bruising in non-mobile infants under 6 months is a sentinel injury with very high specificity for abuse
RCH workup: non contrast CT head plus skeletal survey within 24 hours plus ophthalmology
Mandatory reporting applies to suspicion, not certainty
Normal coagulation studies do NOT exclude inflicted injury
explanation.pearls: TEN-4-FACESp bruising rule: any bruise in a child under 4 months, or torso-ear-neck bruise under 4 years, carries >97% specificity for abuse and triggers immediate CT plus skeletal survey within 24 hours.

sources.label: RACP Position Statement on Child Abuse

stem:
A 14-month-old previously well boy is reviewed on day 7 of fever above 38.5 °C that has not abated despite oral amoxicillin started by a general practitioner on day 4. He is irritable and feeding poorly. His mother reports red eyes for the last four days and a maculopapular trunk rash that came and went on day 5.

On examination he has bilateral non purulent conjunctival injection, fissured red lips with a strawberry tongue, and erythema of the palms with periungual desquamation just starting on the right second finger. There is no cervical lymphadenopathy and no peripheral oedema. Throat swab and urine culture sent on day 4 are negative; a chest radiograph was clear. Echocardiography this morning shows coronary arteries within normal limits for body surface area.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 168/min, BP 90/55 mmHg, RR 32/min, SpO2 98% room air, temperature 39.1 °C
data_table.Investigations: Haemoglobin 95 g/L (105-135); platelets 612 x10^9/L (150-400); WCC 18.2 x10^9/L (5-15); CRP 142 mg/L (<5); ALT 78 U/L (<40); albumin 28 g/L (35-50); sodium 131 mmol/L (135-145); urinalysis sterile pyuria

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Withhold immunoglobulin and observe for 48 hours pending fever resolution
options.rationale: Treatment cannot be deferred because coronary aneurysm risk rises sharply if IVIG is given after day 10 of fever; the laboratory picture already meets incomplete criteria. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Repeat echocardiography in 7 days before deciding on immunoglobulin treatment
options.rationale: A normal initial echocardiogram does NOT rule out Kawasaki; coronary changes often emerge after day 10, by which time IVIG benefit on aneurysm prevention has been lost. (Source: AHA Scientific Statement)

options.text: Switch antibiotic therapy to intravenous ceftriaxone for occult bacterial infection
options.rationale: Cultures are negative and the syndrome fits Kawasaki; antibiotic escalation delays the time critical IVIG window and treats a phantom diagnosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Administer high dose oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg daily as primary monotherapy
options.rationale: Corticosteroid monotherapy is not standard initial therapy in Australia; steroids are added to IVIG only for high risk or refractory disease, not as substitute first line. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease)

options.text: Commence intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg over 10 hours plus oral aspirin
options.rationale: He meets incomplete Kawasaki criteria with supportive laboratory findings (CRP, thrombocytosis, anaemia, hypoalbuminaemia, transaminitis, sterile pyuria); IVIG must be given within 10 days regardless of normal echocardiography. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki Disease; AHA Statement)

explanation.summary: Incomplete Kawasaki is diagnosed when prolonged fever is accompanied by two or three principal features and supportive laboratory findings, even without the full five-criterion picture. Treatment is identical to complete Kawasaki: IVIG 2 g/kg as a single infusion plus aspirin, ideally between day 5 and day 10. Aneurysm rates fall from approximately 25% untreated to 4% with timely IVIG. A normal initial echocardiogram does not exclude diagnosis or remove the treatment indication, because coronary changes may emerge in the convalescent phase. Cardiology follow up at 2 and 6 weeks is mandatory.
key_points: Incomplete Kawasaki: fever ≥5 days plus 2 to 3 features plus supportive labs
Lab support: CRP, ESR, thrombocytosis, anaemia, transaminitis, hypoalbuminaemia, sterile pyuria
IVIG 2 g/kg single infusion plus aspirin within day 10
Steroids added only in high risk or IVIG resistant cases
explanation.pearls: IVIG given after day 10 of fever loses most of its aneurysm-preventive benefit; do not wait for a complete five-criterion picture if labs and clinical features already support incomplete Kawasaki.

stem:
You are the rural ED registrar at a regional hospital seeing a 22-month-old girl brought in 90 minutes after falling backwards off a kitchen chair onto a carpeted floor. The fall was witnessed by her father. There was no loss of consciousness. She vomited once at home and once in triage, then settled in her father's arms and accepted a bottle.

On arrival she is calm, smiles when distracted with bubbles, and her father states she is acting like herself. There is a 4 cm boggy haematoma over the right parietal scalp without overlying laceration. Anterior fontanelle is closed. Pupils are equal and reactive, tone is normal, and she tracks toys with both eyes. There is no palpable skull step. Glasgow coma score is 15.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 122/min, BP 94/58 mmHg, RR 28/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: GCS 15, calm in father's arms, accepts bottle; 4 cm boggy right parietal scalp haematoma; no palpable skull step; pupils equal and reactive
data_table.History: Fall from kitchen chair onto carpet, witnessed, no loss of consciousness, 2 episodes of vomiting, normal behaviour now

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Immediate non contrast CT head and admit under the paediatric team
options.rationale: Non frontal scalp haematoma alone in an otherwise normal child meets PECARN intermediate risk; the PECARN authors recommend observation versus CT based on clinician experience and parental preference, not automatic scan. (Source: PECARN head injury rule)

options.text: Discharge home immediately with written head injury advice for her father
options.rationale: Two episodes of vomiting plus a non frontal boggy haematoma fall into the PECARN intermediate-risk group; same-day discharge without an observation period is unsafe. (Source: PECARN head injury rule)

options.text: Observe in the emergency department for 4 to 6 hours with neurological observations
options.rationale: She has a single intermediate PECARN feature (non frontal scalp haematoma) without LOC, altered mental status, severe mechanism, or palpable skull fracture; PECARN supports a planned observation strategy which halves CT use without missing clinically important traumatic brain injury. (Source: PECARN head injury rule; RCH CPG: Head Injury)

options.text: Arrange a skull radiograph series to exclude a linear parietal fracture
options.rationale: Plain skull radiographs are not part of PECARN or RCH pathways; they neither rule in nor rule out clinically important traumatic brain injury and add radiation without benefit. (Source: RCH CPG: Head Injury)

options.text: Transfer urgently to a tertiary paediatric centre by retrieval team
options.rationale: Retrieval is reserved for GCS deterioration, focal neurology, suspected open or depressed fracture, or positive CT findings; a well child with one intermediate feature does not meet retrieval thresholds. (Source: RCH CPG: Head Injury)

explanation.summary: The PECARN under-2 rule stratifies blunt head injury by mental status, signs of skull fracture, scalp haematoma, loss of consciousness >5 seconds, severe mechanism, and parental concern about behaviour. Children with NO predictors can be discharged. Children with intermediate predictors (non frontal scalp haematoma, LOC <5 s, severe mechanism, abnormal behaviour) have a clinically important TBI rate of approximately 0.9% and can be managed by observation or selective CT based on age, clinician experience, multiple predictors and parental preference. Children with high risk features (GCS <15, altered mental status, palpable skull fracture) need CT.
key_points: PECARN under-2 high risk: GCS <15, altered mental status, palpable skull fracture
PECARN intermediate: non frontal haematoma, LOC <5 s, severe mechanism, abnormal behaviour
Observation halves CT use without missing important injury in intermediate group
Plain skull radiograph is not part of the pathway
explanation.pearls: PECARN under-2 intermediate group has a clinically important TBI rate of ~0.9%; a 4 to 6 hour observation period is supported and avoids unnecessary CT radiation in toddlers.

stem:
You are the paediatric registrar caring for a 9-year-old girl admitted to the ward four hours ago with newly diagnosed type 1 diabetes and diabetic ketoacidosis. She received a 10 mL/kg saline bolus on arrival, was started on 0.05 units/kg/hour intravenous insulin two hours ago, and is now on 0.45% saline plus 5% dextrose with potassium running at her calculated maintenance plus 48-hour deficit replacement rate.

Her mother calls you because the child has just complained of a new severe headache and has vomited once. On review she is drowsy, opens eyes only to voice and her speech is slurred. The bedside nurse reports the blood pressure has risen from 105/65 to 132/82 over the last 30 minutes and the heart rate has fallen from 122 to 88. Pupils are equal and reactive. Sodium has fallen from 138 to 131 since admission.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 88/min, BP 132/82 mmHg, RR 18/min, SpO2 98% room air, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Neurology: Drowsy, GCS 12 (E3 V4 M5), pupils equal and reactive, no focal deficit
data_table.Investigations: BSL 14 mmol/L; sodium 131 mmol/L (135-145, was 138); pH 7.18 (was 7.05); ketones 4.2 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue the current fluid regimen at the same rate and reassess her neurology and pupils in 30 minutes
options.rationale: Headache plus drowsiness plus rising BP plus falling HR in DKA represents Cushing's response from cerebral oedema; passive observation worsens outcomes and delays neuroprotection. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis; ISPAD 2022 guidelines)

options.text: Administer mannitol 0.5 g/kg or hypertonic 3% saline 2.5 mL/kg and reduce IV fluid rate by one third
options.rationale: Suspected cerebral oedema in paediatric DKA mandates immediate hyperosmolar therapy (mannitol 0.5 to 1 g/kg or hypertonic 3% saline 2.5 to 5 mL/kg) plus reduction of fluid rate by one third; do not wait for CT confirmation. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis; ISPAD 2022 guidelines)

options.text: Increase the insulin infusion to 0.1 units/kg/hour and add a second saline bolus
options.rationale: Accelerating insulin and re-bolusing fluid both worsen cerebral oedema by driving an osmotic gradient into the brain; this is the opposite of what is required. (Source: ISPAD 2022 guidelines)

options.text: Stop the insulin infusion entirely and check serum osmolality before any further action
options.rationale: Stopping insulin allows ketogenesis to rebound; with a syndrome of cerebral oedema the clock is short, and osmolality testing should not delay neuroprotective therapy. (Source: ISPAD 2022 guidelines)

options.text: Arrange an urgent non contrast CT head to confirm cerebral oedema before treatment
options.rationale: Cerebral oedema in DKA is a clinical diagnosis; CT is normal in up to 40% of cases at presentation and should NEVER delay mannitol or hypertonic saline. (Source: RCH CPG: Diabetic Ketoacidosis)

explanation.summary: Cerebral oedema complicates approximately 0.5 to 1% of paediatric DKA episodes with mortality up to 25%. The Muir criteria stratify clinical features into diagnostic (abnormal motor response, decorticate posturing, cranial nerve palsy, abnormal breathing pattern), major (altered mental status, sustained heart rate deceleration, age-inappropriate incontinence), and minor (vomiting, headache, lethargy, BP >90th centile, age <5 years). One diagnostic, two major, or one major plus two minor criteria mandate immediate treatment. The Cushing response (rising BP with falling HR) is a late and ominous sign. Hyperosmolar therapy is the time-critical intervention; CT must not delay it.
key_points: Cushing response in DKA = cerebral oedema until proven otherwise
Mannitol 0.5 to 1 g/kg OR hypertonic 3% saline 2.5 to 5 mL/kg immediately
Reduce IV fluid rate by one third concurrently
CT is normal in up to 40%, do NOT delay therapy for imaging
explanation.pearls: Hypertonic 3% saline 2.5 to 5 mL/kg is the preferred first-line hyperosmolar agent in paediatric DKA cerebral oedema per ISPAD 2022; mannitol 0.5 to 1 g/kg is an acceptable alternative.

stem:
A 3-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his grandmother after an episode at home four hours ago where he started coughing suddenly while eating peanuts on the couch and then settled. He has been intermittently coughing since and his grandmother says his breathing sounds noisier on one side.

On arrival he is alert and talking in full sentences. There is no audible stridor. Auscultation reveals reduced air entry and end-expiratory wheeze over the right lower zone with normal breath sounds on the left. There is no fever and no recent viral illness. He has never wheezed before and has no atopy. An inspiratory chest radiograph performed in the department is reported as showing no focal consolidation, no pneumothorax, equal lung volumes and no radio-opaque foreign body.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 122/min, BP 96/60 mmHg, RR 30/min, SpO2 95% room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Talking in full sentences; mild subcostal recession; reduced air entry plus end-expiratory wheeze over right lower zone; left chest clear; no stridor
data_table.Investigations: Inspiratory chest radiograph: no consolidation, no pneumothorax, equal lung volumes, no radio-opaque foreign body

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer for urgent flexible bronchoscopy under sedation in the emergency department
options.rationale: Flexible bronchoscopy is diagnostic only and lacks the working channel for retrieval; once aspiration is suspected the procedure of choice is rigid bronchoscopy in theatre. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreign Body Inhalation)

options.text: Trial salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer and reassess auscultation in 20 minutes
options.rationale: Bronchodilators are appropriate for viral wheeze or asthma but do not address an aspirated foreign body and delay definitive retrieval; persistent unilateral wheeze after choking is the discriminator. (Source: RCH CPG: Wheeze in Children)

options.text: Refer urgently to ENT or paediatric surgery for rigid bronchoscopy under general anaesthesia
options.rationale: History of a choking episode plus unilateral wheeze plus reduced air entry is the classic foreign body triad; a normal inspiratory chest radiograph does NOT exclude an organic foreign body, which is radiolucent in over 80% of cases. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreign Body Inhalation; ANZCA Position Statement)
source_refs: ANZCA Position Statement on Paediatric Airway

options.text: Discharge home with safety net advice and outpatient paediatric review next week
options.rationale: Discharge risks distal migration, post obstructive pneumonia and lobar collapse; once the syndrome is recognised, bronchoscopy is mandatory. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreign Body Inhalation)

options.text: Order a non contrast chest CT to identify the level of the aspirated foreign body
options.rationale: CT has diagnostic utility but should NOT delay rigid bronchoscopy when clinical suspicion is high and bronchoscopy is required regardless of CT findings. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreign Body Inhalation)

explanation.summary: Foreign body aspiration peaks between 1 and 3 years; peanuts, beads, small toys and battery cells are the classic culprits. The diagnostic triad is choking episode plus unilateral wheeze plus reduced air entry on auscultation. A normal chest radiograph does NOT exclude the diagnosis because organic objects such as peanuts are radiolucent in over 80% of cases; expiratory or lateral decubitus films may show air trapping but their absence is not reassuring. Rigid bronchoscopy under general anaesthesia in theatre is both diagnostic and therapeutic and should not be delayed by further imaging. Button battery aspiration is a parallel emergency that demands immediate retrieval given the rapid mucosal injury.
key_points: Triad: choking episode + unilateral wheeze + reduced air entry
Normal CXR does NOT exclude foreign body (radiolucent in >80%)
Rigid bronchoscopy in theatre, not flexible bronchoscopy or CT
Button battery is a parallel emergency requiring immediate retrieval
explanation.pearls: Peanuts and other organic foreign bodies are radiolucent in over 80% of paediatric aspirations; a normal inspiratory chest radiograph must NOT delay rigid bronchoscopy once the choking-plus-unilateral-wheeze pattern is recognised.

sources.label: ANZCA Position Statement on Paediatric Airway

stem:
An 8-year-old known asthmatic boy presents to a metropolitan emergency department with worsening cough and shortness of breath for 12 hours after running outside this morning. He has had asthma since age 4 on twice-daily fluticasone 100 mcg and a salbutamol reliever as needed. He has used his blue puffer six times in the last two hours at home with no sustained improvement.

On arrival he speaks in short phrases, is sitting forward, and uses accessory muscles. Auscultation reveals widespread polyphonic wheeze with reduced air entry bilaterally. He receives three back-to-back nebulised salbutamol 5 mg plus ipratropium 250 mcg combinations over 30 minutes and oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg. After this he remains tachypnoeic with continued accessory muscle use and SpO2 92% on 4 L/min via nasal prongs; he is still speaking in short phrases only.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 138/min, BP 112/68 mmHg, RR 38/min, SpO2 92% on 4 L/min nasal prongs, temperature 37.0 °C
data_table.Examination: Sitting forward, accessory muscle use; widespread polyphonic wheeze, reduced air entry bilaterally; speaks in short phrases
data_table.Medications given: Three back-to-back nebulised salbutamol 5 mg plus ipratropium 250 mcg; oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer intravenous magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg over 20 minutes (max 2 g)
options.rationale: Acute severe asthma not responding to back-to-back salbutamol plus ipratropium plus systemic steroid is the trigger for IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg over 20 minutes per the National Asthma Council and RCH pathway. (Source: National Asthma Council Handbook; RCH CPG: Asthma Acute)

options.text: Add nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000 to the next bronchodilator cycle
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline is reserved for croup and anaphylaxis-related airway oedema, NOT bronchospasm; the bronchodilator escalation in asthma is IV magnesium followed by aminophylline or salbutamol infusion. (Source: National Asthma Council Handbook)

options.text: Increase the inhaled fluticasone dose to 250 mcg twice daily before discharge
options.rationale: Increasing the daily preventer is appropriate at outpatient review, but does not relieve acute severe bronchospasm in the emergency setting. (Source: National Asthma Council Handbook)

options.text: Commence broad spectrum intravenous antibiotics for presumed bacterial chest infection
options.rationale: There is no fever or focal consolidation; antibiotics are not indicated for acute asthma in the absence of infective signs and do not relieve bronchospasm. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma Acute)

options.text: Switch from nasal prong oxygen to high flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min
options.rationale: High flow oxygen alone does not treat bronchospasm; oxygenation supports the work of breathing but the failure point here is airflow obstruction, which requires IV magnesium. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma Acute)

explanation.summary: Acute severe asthma in children is staged as mild, moderate, severe and life-threatening based on speech, work of breathing, oxygen saturation and conscious level. First line is back-to-back nebulised or spacer-delivered salbutamol plus ipratropium plus systemic steroid. Failure to respond after three cycles defines acute severe asthma and triggers IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg over 20 minutes (max 2 g). Further failure escalates to IV salbutamol or aminophylline infusion and PICU referral. Reassessment after each intervention is the heartbeat of acute paediatric asthma care.
key_points: First line: back-to-back salbutamol plus ipratropium plus oral prednisolone
IV magnesium 50 mg/kg over 20 min (max 2 g) for failure to respond
Next escalation: IV salbutamol or aminophylline plus PICU
Antibiotics not indicated without infective signs
explanation.pearls: IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg over 20 minutes (maximum 2 g) is the standard next step in paediatric asthma not responding to three back-to-back nebulised salbutamol plus ipratropium cycles plus systemic steroid.

stem:
A 22-month-old boy is brought to your general practice clinic by his mother for a third episode of audible wheeze in the past 6 months. Each episode followed a runny nose by 24 to 48 hours and lasted 4 to 5 days. Between episodes he is completely well and runs around without breathlessness. He has had no nocturnal wheeze. There is no family history of asthma, eczema or hay fever, and he has had no admissions.

On examination today he is afebrile, talking in normal phrases for his age, and his work of breathing is normal. Auscultation reveals scattered end expiratory wheeze with normal air entry bilaterally. His mother has been giving 4 puffs of salbutamol via a small-volume spacer at the onset of each cold which she says reduces symptoms within 30 minutes. He has had no salbutamol use today.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 118/min, BP 92/56 mmHg, RR 26/min, SpO2 98% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Talking in normal phrases for age, no accessory muscle use; scattered end expiratory wheeze; normal air entry; afebrile
data_table.History: 3 episodes wheeze in 6 months, each cold-triggered, well between episodes; no nocturnal wheeze; no atopy; salbutamol responsive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Commence inhaled corticosteroid fluticasone 50 mcg twice daily as a daily preventer
options.rationale: Daily ICS is not first-line for episodic viral wheeze in a child this age without interval symptoms or atopy; reassessment at age 5 is the better strategy. (Source: National Asthma Council Handbook; RCH CPG: Wheeze)

options.text: Trial montelukast 4 mg orally each evening for 12 weeks then reassess
options.rationale: Montelukast has marginal benefit in episodic viral wheeze and now carries a TGA boxed warning for neuropsychiatric adverse events; it is not first-line in this scenario. (Source: AMH; TGA Safety Advisory Montelukast)

options.text: Continue salbutamol as needed via spacer and review again if symptoms change
options.rationale: Episodic viral wheeze in a 1 to 3 year old who is well between episodes and lacks atopy is best managed with as-needed bronchodilator via spacer; daily preventer is reserved for frequent episodic, persistent, or atopy-positive children. (Source: National Asthma Council Handbook; RCH CPG: Wheeze)

options.text: Refer for skin prick allergen testing to common inhaled and food allergens
options.rationale: Skin prick testing has low yield without interval symptoms or other atopic features and would not change today's management. (Source: ASCIA Position Statement on Allergy Testing)
source_refs: ASCIA Position Statement on Allergy Testing

options.text: Prescribe an oral prednisolone course for use at the start of each viral episode
options.rationale: Parent-initiated oral steroids at viral onset do NOT prevent attacks in preschool wheeze and have been deprescribed in current Australian guidelines. (Source: National Asthma Council Handbook)

explanation.summary: Preschool wheeze splits into episodic viral wheeze (well between, no atopy) and multi-trigger wheeze (interval symptoms, atopy, family history). Episodic viral wheeze is managed with as-needed salbutamol via spacer; daily inhaled corticosteroid is reserved for frequent or persistent episodes, multi-trigger pattern, or strong atopy where the modified Asthma Predictive Index is positive. Montelukast has limited efficacy and is now boxed-warning for neuropsychiatric effects. Most children with episodic viral wheeze stop wheezing by school age and a planned reassessment around age 5 differentiates persistent asthma.
key_points: Episodic viral wheeze in 1 to 3 year olds without atopy: salbutamol via spacer as needed
Daily ICS reserved for frequent/persistent or atopy-positive patterns
Montelukast has TGA neuropsychiatric boxed warning, low first-line value
Most episodic viral wheezers resolve by school age
explanation.pearls: The modified Asthma Predictive Index (mAPI) positive picture is frequent wheeze plus one major criterion (parent asthma, eczema, aeroallergen sensitisation) or two minor criteria (food allergy, eosinophilia ≥4%, wheeze without cold) and predicts persistent asthma.

sources.label: ASCIA Position Statement on Allergy Testing

stem:
You are the neonatal registrar reviewing a 32 week, 1.4 kg male preterm infant now 12 days old who has had bloody stools and bilious aspirates for the last 18 hours. He had been tolerating expressed breast milk via gavage at 120 mL/kg/day until two days ago. He was started on broad spectrum antibiotics and bowel rest 12 hours ago and has had an umbilical arterial catheter inserted for serial gases.

Over the last 6 hours his platelet count has fallen from 145 to 62 x10^9/L and lactate has risen from 1.8 to 5.4 mmol/L. He is now intubated and on dopamine 10 mcg/kg/min for mean arterial pressure 28 mmHg. His abdomen is tense, discoloured and quiet to auscultation. The on-call radiographer reports the upright abdominal radiograph shows extensive pneumatosis intestinalis, portal venous gas and a small amount of free intra-abdominal gas in the right upper quadrant.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 178/min, MAP 28 mmHg on dopamine, RR 60/min ventilated, SpO2 92% on 60% FiO2, temperature 35.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Tense discoloured abdomen with absent bowel sounds; mottled lower limbs; CRT 5 seconds
data_table.Investigations: Hb 88 g/L (140-220); platelets 62 x10^9/L (was 145); lactate 5.4 mmol/L (was 1.8); pH 7.18; base excess -12; CRP 142 mg/L
data_table.Imaging: Upright abdominal radiograph: extensive pneumatosis intestinalis, portal venous gas, free gas in right upper quadrant

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Urgent paediatric surgical referral for laparotomy or peritoneal drain insertion
options.rationale: Free intraperitoneal gas in necrotising enterocolitis is an absolute Bell stage IIIB surgical indication; immediate paediatric surgical involvement is mandatory, with the choice between primary laparotomy and primary peritoneal drainage guided by birth weight (<1000 g often drained first) and stability. (Source: RANZCO Neonatal Surgery Guidelines; Royal Hospital for Women Neonatal Handbook)
source_refs: Royal Hospital for Women Neonatal Handbook

options.text: Continue medical management with bowel rest, antibiotics and inotropic support
options.rationale: Pneumoperitoneum is an absolute surgical indication regardless of clinical stability; ongoing medical management alone risks lethal sepsis from contained perforation. (Source: RANZCO Neonatal Surgery Guidelines; Royal Hospital for Women Neonatal Handbook)
source_refs: Royal Hospital for Women Neonatal Handbook

options.text: Add intravenous immunoglobulin 1 g/kg as adjunctive therapy for severe sepsis
options.rationale: IVIG has no proven role in necrotising enterocolitis or neonatal sepsis and does not address the surgical perforation indicated by free gas. (Source: Royal Hospital for Women Neonatal Handbook)
source_refs: Royal Hospital for Women Neonatal Handbook

options.text: Repeat the abdominal radiograph in 6 hours to confirm progression before referral
options.rationale: Free gas already establishes the surgical indication; repeating imaging delays definitive treatment and risks decompensation. (Source: RANZCO Neonatal Surgery Guidelines)

options.text: Switch enteral feeds to a hydrolysed amino acid formula via continuous nasogastric infusion
options.rationale: Any enteral feeding is contraindicated with proven NEC and free gas; this question is surgical not nutritional. (Source: Royal Hospital for Women Neonatal Handbook)
source_refs: Royal Hospital for Women Neonatal Handbook

explanation.summary: Necrotising enterocolitis is staged by the modified Bell criteria from suspected (I) through definite medical NEC (II) to advanced NEC with intestinal perforation or impending perforation (III). Free intraperitoneal gas defines Bell IIIB and is an absolute surgical indication. In extremely low birth weight infants (<1000 g) primary peritoneal drainage may be preferred as a bridge to laparotomy. Pneumatosis intestinalis and portal venous gas without free gas are managed medically with bowel rest, broad spectrum antibiotics (typically ampicillin plus gentamicin plus metronidazole) and supportive care. Mortality in Bell IIIB approaches 50% in extremely preterm infants and survivors face short gut syndrome.
key_points: Bell IIIB = perforation = surgical, regardless of stability
Pneumatosis alone or portal venous gas alone = Bell II = medical management
Extremely low birth weight (<1000 g) may have primary peritoneal drainage
Antibiotics: ampicillin plus gentamicin plus metronidazole
explanation.pearls: Pneumoperitoneum in NEC mandates immediate surgical referral; in infants under 1000 g, primary peritoneal drainage at the bedside under local anaesthesia is an evidence-supported temporising option per the NET trial when laparotomy is not immediately feasible.

sources.label: Royal Hospital for Women Neonatal Handbook

stem:
You are the antenatal clinic registrar at a regional hospital seeing a 33-year-old G2P1 woman at 36 weeks for a planning discussion about her mode of birth. Her previous birth was an emergency lower segment caesarean section at term two years ago for arrest of dilation at 6 cm despite syntocinon augmentation; the baby was 3.6 kg and the operation note describes a transverse lower segment incision with no extension. She has had no other pregnancies.

This pregnancy has been uncomplicated. The foetus is cephalic, estimated foetal weight on a growth scan two days ago is 3.4 kg with normal umbilical artery dopplers, and the placenta is fundal. Her body mass index at booking was 28 kg/m2. She has had no interpregnancy interval issues and is keen to attempt a vaginal birth. The regional hospital has a 24/7 obstetrician on call, an in-house anaesthetist, blood bank availability, and a 30 minute decision-to-delivery audit.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78/min, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 16/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C, foetal heart 142/min
data_table.Examination: Symphysis fundal height 36 cm, longitudinal lie, cephalic, free 4/5; uterus soft and non tender
data_table.History: G2P1; previous LSCS at term for arrest of dilation; transverse lower segment incision; current EFW 3.4 kg; BMI 28

lead_in: What is the most appropriate counselling recommendation?

options.text: Advise that vaginal birth is contraindicated because of her body mass index of 28 kg/m2
options.rationale: A BMI of 28 is not a contraindication to VBAC; obesity reduces success rates but does not preclude trial of labour. (Source: RANZCOG Birth After Previous Caesarean Section)
source_refs: RANZCOG Birth After Previous Caesarean Section

options.text: Recommend elective repeat caesarean section at 39 weeks given prior arrest of dilation
options.rationale: Prior labour arrest is not an absolute contraindication to VBAC; success rates fall modestly but remain above 50% and an elective ERCS removes maternal choice based on a non-absolute factor. (Source: RANZCOG Birth After Previous Caesarean Section)
source_refs: RANZCOG Birth After Previous Caesarean Section

options.text: Induce labour at 39 weeks with prostaglandin gel to optimise her chance of vaginal birth
options.rationale: Prostaglandin induction of VBAC is associated with a 2 to 3 fold rise in uterine rupture compared with spontaneous labour and is generally avoided; mechanical induction (Foley) is preferred if induction is needed. (Source: RANZCOG Birth After Previous Caesarean Section)
source_refs: RANZCOG Birth After Previous Caesarean Section

options.text: Offer trial of labour after caesarean with continuous CTG and IV access from established labour
options.rationale: She is a good VBAC candidate (single prior low transverse LSCS, no other uterine surgery, cephalic presentation, average EFW, supportive hospital infrastructure); RANZCOG supports VBAC with continuous CTG once in established labour and intravenous access, with reported success rates 60-80% and uterine rupture risk approximately 0.5%. (Source: RANZCOG Birth After Previous Caesarean Section)
source_refs: RANZCOG Birth After Previous Caesarean Section

options.text: Arrange external cephalic version at 37 weeks before any further mode of birth discussion
options.rationale: The foetus is already cephalic; ECV is not indicated and does not change the VBAC discussion. (Source: RANZCOG External Cephalic Version)

explanation.summary: Trial of labour after caesarean is supported by RANZCOG for women with a single prior low transverse LSCS, no other uterine scar, cephalic presentation, no contraindication to vaginal birth and access to facilities able to perform emergency caesarean within 30 minutes. Reported success rates are 60-80% and uterine rupture risk is approximately 0.5%. Continuous CTG is mandatory in established labour because foetal heart rate change is the earliest sign of rupture. Prostaglandin induction is generally avoided due to elevated rupture risk; mechanical methods are preferred. Two prior caesareans, classical scar, T or J incision, or low transverse extension are absolute contraindications.
key_points: VBAC candidate: 1 prior low transverse LSCS, cephalic, supportive infrastructure
Uterine rupture risk approximately 0.5% in spontaneous VBAC labour
Continuous CTG mandatory from established labour onward
Avoid prostaglandin induction; mechanical (Foley) preferred
explanation.pearls: RANZCOG recommends decision-to-delivery within 30 minutes for category 1 emergency caesarean; this is the critical infrastructure prerequisite that allows VBAC at a regional hospital.

sources.label: RANZCOG Birth After Previous Caesarean Section

stem:
You are the obstetric registrar called to the assessment unit at 11pm for a 28-year-old G1P0 woman at 32+4 weeks brought in by her partner with a frontal headache for 6 hours and three episodes of vomiting. She has been seeing a midwife at a low-risk shared care clinic. Booking BP was 110/70 mmHg.

On arrival she is reactive but withdrawn. Repeat manual BP measurements over 15 minutes are 168/112, 172/108 and 165/110 mmHg. Reflexes are brisk with 3 beats of ankle clonus. There is no visual scotoma. Urine dipstick shows protein 3+. CTG shows a baseline of 145/min with normal variability and accelerations, no decelerations. The bed manager confirms a tertiary neonatal unit cot is available 90 minutes away by retrieval.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 98/min, BP 168/112 mmHg, RR 18/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C, foetal heart 145/min
data_table.Examination: Reactive, withdrawn; brisk reflexes with 3 beats clonus; no scotoma; no epigastric tenderness; SFH 33 cm
data_table.Investigations: Urine dipstick protein 3+; platelets 142 x10^9/L (150-400); ALT 78 U/L (<40); creatinine 88 micromol/L; uric acid raised; PlGF assay pending
data_table.CTG: Baseline 145/min, normal variability, accelerations present, no decelerations

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Administer intravenous frusemide 40 mg as a single bolus for pulmonary oedema prophylaxis
options.rationale: Routine frusemide is not part of acute severe pre-eclampsia management and may worsen placental perfusion; it is reserved for confirmed pulmonary oedema. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Commence betamethasone 11.4 mg IM and plan delivery in the morning by elective caesarean
options.rationale: Antenatal corticosteroid is correct at this gestation, but delaying acute BP control and magnesium until morning risks eclampsia or stroke; both are time critical. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Oral nifedipine 20 mg immediate-release and IV magnesium sulfate 4 g loading dose, then arrange retrieval
options.rationale: Severe hypertension (BP ≥160/110) plus pre-eclampsia symptoms requires immediate acute antihypertensive (oral nifedipine 10 to 20 mg or IV labetalol 20 mg) plus magnesium sulfate 4 g loading then 1 g/hour for eclampsia prophylaxis; tertiary in utero transfer is appropriate at 32+4 weeks if mother stabilises. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy; RANZCOG Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Discharge home on oral labetalol 100 mg three times daily with day-stay review tomorrow
options.rationale: Severe hypertension with symptoms and proteinuria is an admission with magnesium prophylaxis; home management at this severity is unsafe. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Start an intravenous glyceryl trinitrate infusion targeting mean arterial pressure under 80 mmHg
options.rationale: GTN is reserved for pre-eclampsia with pulmonary oedema; first-line acute antihypertensives are oral nifedipine or IV labetalol or IV hydralazine. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Pre-eclampsia with severe features (BP ≥160/110, symptoms of impending eclampsia such as headache, scotoma, epigastric pain, hyperreflexia, or end-organ involvement) requires admission, immediate acute antihypertensive, magnesium sulfate for eclampsia prophylaxis, and a delivery plan. The SOMANZ first-line acute agents are oral nifedipine 10 to 20 mg, IV labetalol 20 mg (escalating), or IV hydralazine 5 mg. Magnesium sulfate 4 g IV loading then 1 g/hour infusion halves the risk of eclampsia. At pre-term gestations between 24 and 34 weeks, antenatal corticosteroids and consideration of in utero transfer to a tertiary centre are part of the package. Delivery timing depends on maternal and foetal stability; at 32 to 34 weeks, expectant management is appropriate if both are stable.
key_points: Severe BP threshold = 160/110 mmHg
Acute antihypertensive: nifedipine PO or labetalol IV or hydralazine IV
Magnesium sulfate 4 g IV load then 1 g/hour for eclampsia prophylaxis
Tertiary transfer at 32+ weeks if mother and foetus stable
explanation.pearls: Magnesium sulfate halves eclampsia risk in severe pre-eclampsia (NNT approximately 100); monitor patellar reflex, respiratory rate and urine output and check magnesium level if signs of toxicity emerge.
